title: How to Successfully Appeal a Standardized Test Score Reporting Error
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You took the SAT or ACT. You prepared for months. Your practice test scores were consistently strong. Then the official scores arrived, and they are significantly lower than you expected. Something does not add up. You check your account and notice a discrepancy. Maybe the testing portal shows a different score than the official report. Maybe a section is missing entirely. Maybe your test was flagged for irregularities you did not commit. Standardized testing organizations process millions of exams annually, and errors happen. When they do, you have the right to appeal. Here is how to navigate the appeals process effectively.

Determine If You Have Grounds for an Appeal

Not all disappointing scores are errors. A score that is lower than you hoped is not grounds for an appeal. The testing organizations have rigorous scoring procedures, and simple score dissatisfaction is not a valid basis for a dispute. Valid grounds for appeal include a significant discrepancy between your online score report and the official score sent to colleges, a test center irregularity such as a fire alarm, power outage, or proctor error that disrupted testing conditions, a scoring anomaly such as a section score that is drastically inconsistent with your other sections in a way that suggests a bubbling or scanning error, or an administrative error such as your test being incorrectly flagged for time violations or academic dishonesty when you followed all procedures.

Before you begin an appeal, gather evidence. Print your score report. Screenshot any online discrepancies. Write down exactly what you experienced if the issue involved testing conditions. The more specific and documented your claim, the more seriously it will be taken.

Contact the Testing Organization Promptly

Time matters. Both the College Board, which administers the SAT, and ACT Inc. have strict deadlines for filing appeals. For score verification, you typically have a window of a few months after your test date. For test center irregularities, you should report the issue immediately, ideally on test day or within a few days.

Start by calling customer service. Explain your situation clearly and ask what specific form or process applies. Write down the name of every person you speak with and the date and time of each call. Ask for a case number or reference number. This documentation trail protects you if the process stalls.

Follow up the phone call with a written complaint through the official channels. Both the College Board and ACT have online forms for reporting testing irregularities. Fill them out completely. Attach any documentation you have. Be factual, not emotional. A clear, documented claim is more effective than an angry one.

Understand the Score Verification Options

The SAT offers two levels of score review. Score verification involves hand-scoring your answer sheet to confirm the machine scanned it correctly. This is the appropriate option if you believe there was a bubbling or scanning error. A student answer service provides a copy of the test questions, your answers, and the correct answers, allowing you to review where you made mistakes. The ACT offers similar services through their test information release.

These services cost money, typically between fifty and sixty dollars. If an error is found, the fee is refunded and your score is corrected. If no error is found, you do not get the money back. Consider whether the potential score change is worth the cost. If your score is wildly inconsistent with your practice tests and you have a specific reason to suspect an error, the fee is likely worth it. If your score is disappointing but within a plausible range, the verification will probably confirm the original score.

What to Do If the Appeal Is Denied

Many appeals are denied. The testing organizations have confidence in their scoring systems, and most score discrepancies have explanations other than error. If your appeal is denied, you have several options. You can retake the test. Many students perform better on a second attempt simply because they are more familiar with the format and less anxious. Most colleges superscore, meaning they consider your highest section scores across multiple test dates. A retake can replace the score you are unhappy with.

If your score was affected by a testing irregularity that the testing organization will not address, contact the colleges you applied to directly. Explain what happened. Colleges understand that standardized testing conditions are not always perfect. Some will accept an alternative test date or waive the score requirement if you can document the irregularity.

If the issue is genuinely egregious and the testing organization is unresponsive, you can escalate. Contact the consumer protection division of your state attorney general's office. File a complaint with the Better Business Bureau. These escalations are rarely necessary but can be effective when a large organization is ignoring a legitimate problem.

Prevent Future Errors

On your next test, take precautions. Arrive early at the test center to avoid last-minute stress that could affect your performance. Verify your personal information on the answer sheet before submitting it. Listen carefully to the proctor's instructions and report any irregularities during the test, not after. Order the question-and-answer service when you register for the SAT or ACT. This gives you access to your actual test booklet and answer sheet, which makes it easier to identify errors if your score is surprising.

The Bottom Line

Standardized test score errors are rare, but they happen. If you have specific, documented evidence of an error, act quickly. Contact the testing organization by phone, then follow up in writing. Use the official score verification service if appropriate. If your appeal is denied, retake the test and inform your colleges. One test score does not define your college prospects. Persistence and a clear plan will get you past this obstacle.
